questions.question: Explain the C++ stream class hierarchy starting from ios_base. How are the file stream classes (ifstream, ofstream, fstream) positioned in this hierarchy?
keyPoints: ios_base is the root class providing format flags, locales, error state
ios class derives from ios_base adding streambuf association and error state functions
istream derives from ios for input operations
ostream derives from ios for output operations
iostream inherits from both istream and ostream for bidirectional I/O
ifstream inherits from istream for file input
ofstream inherits from ostream for file output
fstream inherits from iostream for file input/output

questions.question: Differentiate between the following file opening modes: ios::app, ios::ate, ios::trunc, and ios::binary. Give suitable examples of when each would be used.
keyPoints: ios::app - opens for output, positions at end, all writes appended
ios::ate - opens, positions at end initially but allows seeking
ios::trunc - discards existing content if file exists (default for ofstream)
ios::binary - no newline translation, raw byte access
app used for log files, ate for random access updates
trunc for fresh start, binary for non-text data like images

questions.question: What is the streambuf class? Explain its role in the C++ I/O system with reference to the relationship between ios and streambuf.
keyPoints: streambuf provides low-level buffer management for streams
It handles physical storage and retrieval of characters
The ios class contains a pointer to a streambuf object
Data flows through the buffer for efficient I/O operations
get area for input buffering, put area for output buffering
Derived classes like filebuf implement device-specific buffering

questions.question: Describe the error state flags in C++ streams. How would you check for end-of-file condition and differentiate it from a read failure?
keyPoints: Four main flags: goodbit (no errors), eofbit (end reached), failbit (logical error), badbit (fatal error)
good() returns true only if no error bits are set
eof() returns true when eofbit is set - happens AFTER reading past EOF
fail() returns true for failbit or badbit - indicates operation failure
Always check fail() first, then eof() - don't use eof() to control read loops
Correct pattern: while(stream >> variable) processes successfully

questions.question: Explain the difference between cin/cout, cerr, and clog. When would you use each?
keyPoints: cin - istream connected to standard input (keyboard)
cout - ostream connected to standard output (console)
cerr - ostream to standard error, unbuffered, for error messages
clog - ostream to standard error, buffered version of cerr
Use cerr for immediate error output (critical errors)
Use clog for diagnostic messages that can be buffered
cerr and clog can be redirected separately from cout

questions.question: What are stream manipulators? List at least five commonly used manipulators and explain their effects.
keyPoints: Manipulators are functions that modify stream behavior when inserted/extracted
endl - outputs newline and flushes output buffer
setw(n) - sets minimum field width for next output only
setprecision(n) - sets floating-point precision
fixed/scientific - controls floating-point notation
hex/oct/dec - changes integer base for integer output
left/right/internal - controls output alignment
boolalpha/noboolalpha - outputs bool as true/false or 1/0

questions.question: Write a C++ program to open a file in different modes and explain the behavior. What happens when you try to read from a file opened only for output?
keyPoints: Output-only file (ofstream) - cannot read, read operations fail
Input-only file (ifstream) - cannot write, write operations fail
fstream with ios::in|ios::out - both operations allowed
Opening non-existent file for input sets failbit
Opening for output creates file if it doesn't exist
Reading from write-only stream returns failbit
Attempting to read beyond EOF triggers eofbit after the failed read

questions.question: Design and implement a complete C++ program that demonstrates file handling operations including: creating a file, writing data, reading data, checking file state (eof, fail, good), and proper error handling. The program should use ifstream, ofstream, and fstream classes appropriately.
keyPoints: Create and open file with ofstream in text mode
Write various data types using << operator
Close and reopen with ifstream for reading
Use fail(), eof(), good() to check states
Handle errors appropriately with proper checks
Demonstrate read operations including get(), read()
Show binary mode file operations with ios::binary
Proper resource management with close() or RAII

questions.question: Explain the concept of formatted and unformatted I/O in C++. Discuss the member functions and operators used for each type, and explain when you would choose one over the other.
keyPoints: Formatted I/O: uses >> and << operators with automatic type conversion
Handles automatic conversion between internal and text representation
Unformatted I/O: uses get(), getline(), read(), write() for raw byte access
Formatted is convenient for human-readable output and simple input
Unformatted is needed for binary data, exact byte control, performance
get() reads single char including whitespace
read() reads block of bytes without formatting
getline() reads until delimiter
write() writes raw bytes without formatting
Binary files MUST use unformatted I/O to preserve exact byte values

questions.question: A banking application needs to maintain transaction records in a file. The file should support both sequential appending of new transactions and random access to view specific past transactions. Design the file opening strategy and implement the necessary C++ code with proper error handling.
keyPoints: Use ios::app for sequential appending at end of file
Use ios::ate for initial positioning at end but allows seeking
Implement record-based storage with fixed-size structures
Use seekg() for repositioning to specific records
fstream with ios::in|ios::out for read/write access
Check all operations with fail() and handle errors
Consider binary mode for exact record sizes
Design transaction record structure with fixed fields
Implement functions: appendTransaction(), viewTransaction(n)
Proper error messages for file not found, read failures, etc.
